subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the stats exposed via /proc/vmstat are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not CONFIG_MEMCG, and suggested using mod_node_page_state to make global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.
reviews.raw_body:
Hello JP!

The stats are exposed via /proc/vmstat and are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not
CONFIG_MEMCG. Early returning overhere would make it inaccuate. Does
it make sense to use mod_node_page_state if memcg is not available,
so that these global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.

reviews.author: JP (Meta) (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about inconsistent behavior when memcg is disabled, proposed an if-else statement to handle this case and update stats accordingly.

2026-03-08.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

2026-03-07.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern about the number of new metrics added per-memcg, citing a significant memory cost for each additional metric.
reviews.raw_body:
I have not looked into what these metrics mean but these are too many, at least
for the memcg. For the memcg, there is significant memory cost for each metric
added to memcg.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that the new page allocation counters are monotonic increasing metrics, suggesting they are more useful as a rate of change rather than their absolute value, and asked if tracepoints and BPF could be used to achieve this instead.
reviews.raw_body:
This seems like monotonic increasing metrics and I think you don't care about
their absolute value but rather rate of change. Any reason this can not be
achieved through tracepoints and BPF combination?

reviews.author: JP (Meta) (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged Shakeel's concern about the placement of new statistics, explaining they are intended for use alongside existing per-node reclaim stats and would be collected from the same source.
reviews.raw_body:
We have the per-node reclaim stats (pg{steal,scan,refill}) in
nodeN/vmstat and memory.numa_stat now. The new stats in this patch would
be collected from the same source. They were meant to be used together,
so it seemed like a reasonable location. I think the advantage over
tracepoints is we get the observability on from the start and it would
be simple to extend existing programs that already read stats from the
cgroup dir files.

2026-03-09.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner
reviews.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the patch does not handle the !CONFIG_MEMCG case correctly in mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state(), and suggested adding a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() to simplify the code.
reviews.raw_body:
mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state() already do the right thing
for !CONFIG_MEMCG. Add a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() and you can
do a single, shared sequence for both configs.

2026-03-11.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern that adding 18 counters for per-memcg tracking would be too costly, estimating a single counter's cost at 200KiB to MiBs on typical production machines, and suggested decoupling these stats from the rstat infrastructure to reduce overhead.
reviews.raw_body:
Convenience is not really justifying the cost of adding 18 counters,
particularly in memcg. We can argue about adding just in system level metrics
but not for memcg.

counter_cost = nr_cpus * nr_nodes * nr_memcg * 16 (struct lruvec_stats_percpu)

On a typical prod machine, we can see 1000s of memcg, 100s of cpus and couple of
numa nodes. So, a single counter's cost can range from 200KiB to MiBs. This does
not seem like a cost we should force everyone to pay.

If you really want these per-memcg and assuming these metrics are updated in
non-performance critical path, we can try to decouple these and other reclaim
related stats from rstat infra. That would at least reduce nr_cpus factor in the
above equation to 1. Though we will need to actually evaluate the performance
for the change before committing to it.

reviews.author: JP (Meta) (author)
reviews.summary: Author is willing to compromise on per-cgroup granularity, suggesting a change from tracking page allocations per mempolicy per-memcg to globally per-node statistics.
reviews.raw_body:
I could trade off the per-cgroup granularity and change these stats to
become global per-node stats.

2026-03-10.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

reviews.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
reviews.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil expressed concerns that adding more counters (e.g., for preferred vs preferred_many policies) does not provide a clear benefit and may be redundant compared to tracing, requesting alternative solutions.
reviews.raw_body:
I think I've been on of the folks on previous versions arguing against the
many counters, and one of the arguments was it they can't tell the full
story anyway (compared to e.g. tracing), but I don't think adding even more
counters is the right solution. Seems like a number of other people
responding to the thread are providing similar feedback.

For example I'm still not sure how it would help me if I knew the
hits/misses were due to a preferred vs preferred_many policy, or interleave
vs weithed interleave?

reviews.author: JP (Meta) (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about the number and complexity of policy-specific page allocation counters, proposing to simplify the implementation by using a single aggregated policy triplet (hit/miss/foreign) per policy instead of triplets for each policy.
reviews.raw_body:
How about I change from per-policy hit/miss/foreign triplets to a single
aggregated policy triplet (i.e. just 3 new counters which account for
all policies)? They would follow the same hit/miss/foreign semantics
already proposed (visible in quoted text above). This would still
provide the otherwise missing signal of whether policy-driven
allocations to a node are intentional or fallback.

Note that I am also planning on moving the stats off of the memcg so the
3 new counters will be global per-node in response to similar feedback.

2026-03-12.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

reviews.author: JP Kobryn (Meta)
reviews.summary: Proposed simplifying policy triplet counters to a single aggregated counter per node, addressing concerns about signal and stats placement.

2026-03-13.patch_summary: JP Kobryn suggests simplifying the policy triplet counters to a single aggregated counter per node, addressing concerns about signal and stats placement.
